findings.rationale: Our property covers all technical performance specifications, while the upstream property addresses only a specific energy‑efficiency class.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a container linking to a structured TechnicalSpecification covering capacity/voltage/power/lifetime, whereas the upstream property is a single literal naming the top EU energy-efficiency class, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the initial internal resistance measured at commissioning, expressed in Ohms.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a battery's initial internal resistance and almost certainly the same concept by name, but the upstream term has no definition or stated unit/range, so interchangeable EXACT equivalence cannot be confirmed and the weaker CLOSE grade is safer.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the initial round‑trip energy efficiency of a system.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote initial round-trip energy efficiency, but with no upstream definition to confirm identical units, scope, and measurement basis the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: The upstream property denotes a general certification standard, while our term specifies the particular calculation standard used for carbon‑footprint assessment.
findings.qaRationale: A carbon-footprint calculation methodology standard (e.g., ISO 14067) is a different concept from a product certification standard a product is certified against, so it is not a narrower kind of certificationStandard.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of a battery’s rated maximum power output.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI is identical to OUR term's own IRI (eubat: = https://ref.openepcis.io/extensions/eu/battery/), so this is a self-reference, not a distinct upstream concept to align with.

findings.rationale: Both describe engine power in kW, but our property specifies the rated maximum value, a more specific concept than the general enginePower.
findings.qaRationale: schema:enginePower is scoped to a vehicle's engine power, whereas our term is a battery's rated maximum power output; sharing the kW unit is measurement similarity, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a maximum power value for the battery, but one specifies the rated output while the other denotes a permitted limit.
findings.qaRationale: Our 'rated maximum power output' is a nameplate design specification, whereas the undefined upstream 'powerMaximumPermitted' denotes a permitted/regulatory ceiling, a distinct concept, so absent any upstream definition the match cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a particular lifecycle stage for emissions data, whereas schema.org/stage is a generic condition stage.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a string-valued lifecycle phase label for carbon footprint emissions, whereas schema.org/stage refers to the stage of a medical/health condition, so they are unrelated concepts despite the shared name.

findings.rationale: The class describes a specific power measurement at a given state of charge, which is a more detailed instance of the general energy‑consumption information represented by EnergyConsumptionDetails.
findings.qaRationale: Battery power-capability measured at a specific state of charge is a performance metric unrelated to schema.org's product energy-efficiency labeling concept, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies a particular measurement condition, whereas the candidate is a generic evaluation criterion.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is a battery power-capability measurement at a specific state of charge, which is a physical performance datum, not a SEMICeu evaluation condition; a measurement is not a subtype of an assessment criterion.

findings.rationale: The candidate property represents a general recycled content value, whereas our term specifies the share of cobalt pre‑consumer recycled material.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific specialization (single material cobalt, single pre-consumer stream) of the upstream generic recycled-content measure, so it is properly narrower than the candidate.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the total capacity throughput over a battery’s lifetime, expressed as a quantitative value.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific projected lifetime value, whereas the undefined upstream capacityThroughput is an unqualified, likely broader throughput property, so ours is the narrower concept rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the total amount of energy that can be delivered over time, but our term specifies battery lifetime capacity throughput in ampere‑hours whereas the candidate is a more generic energy throughput.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures charge throughput in ampere-hours (Ah) over battery lifetime, while the candidate measures energy throughput (implicitly Wh/energy units) — different physical quantities that are not interchangeable, so they are distinct concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of round‑trip efficiency degradation expressed as a percentage.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares an identical, highly specific name strongly implying the same battery RTE-degradation concept, but with no upstream definition the exact computation, units, and value-range cannot be confirmed interchangeable, so a weaker close match is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our property describes overall RTE degradation over the battery’s lifetime, whereas the candidate refers to a single efficiency value at 50 cycles.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a lifetime RTE-degradation percentage while the candidate is a single round-trip-efficiency reading at 50 cycles; they are different measures of related concepts, but if forced onto a graded SKOS axis our fade metric is the more specific derived quantity, so NARROW at best and arguably NONE.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property refers to general sustainability and recycling information, whereas our term specifies the quantitative carbon‑footprint contribution from the recycling phase.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a single quantitative carbon-footprint metric for the recycling phase, far narrower than GS1's general link to sustainability and recycling information, so it is at most a broadMatch (NARROW) to the GS1 property.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the maximum power rating of a battery, but the candidate term does not specify that it is for discharging only.
findings.qaRationale: Maximum discharging power is a distinct battery parameter from a general rated maximum power, and the undefined candidate gives no basis to treat them as the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the maximum power that a battery is allowed to deliver, though the candidate term does not explicitly restrict it to discharging only.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is restricted to discharging power, whereas the undefined upstream 'powerMaximumPermitted' reads as a generic maximum permitted power, making ours a narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: eubat:hasBattery is a specific subproperty of schema:hasPart, thus it is narrower than the general hasPart property.
findings.qaRationale: Our property restricts the part to a battery (range gs1:Product, semantically a battery), making it narrower than schema:hasPart, so from our term's perspective relative to the upstream general property the correct graded relation is that ours is narrower than hasPart — but the SKOS direction from OUR perspective is that hasBattery is a specialization, hence NARROW.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the percentage of capacity loss that signifies end‑of‑life, but our term specifies a threshold value while the upstream term is more general.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the configured end-of-life threshold value (a spec limit, typically 80%), whereas the upstream 'capacityFade' names the actual capacity-loss measurement itself, so they denote different properties despite the name overlap.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the capacity threshold at which a battery is considered exhausted or reaches end of life.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties denote the end-of-life capacity threshold of a battery, but the undefined upstream term may express an absolute capacity value rather than our fade percentage, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the percentage of a battery’s weight that can be recycled, indicating identical scope.
findings.qaRationale: Our term scopes recyclability to a battery's weight under eubat:EndOfLifeInfo, whereas the upstream oec: core property is the generic cross-cutting recyclability rate, making ours a narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the European Community (EINECS/ELINCS) identifier for a chemical substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote the EINECS/ELINCS European Community chemical identifier so the property semantics align, but ours is domain-restricted to eubat:BatteryMaterial while the upstream core property is general and carries no definition to confirm full interchangeability, so EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: Both refer to energy throughput, but the upstream term lacks the explicit lifetime scope present in our definition.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific lifetime-scoped energy throughput measure that overlaps with the undefined upstream energyThroughput but cannot be confirmed interchangeable given the missing upstream definition, so a weaker CLOSE is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to a measure of energy throughput, but the upstream term lacks the explicit lifetime scope and unit specification present in our definition.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a lifetime energy quantity in kWh while the upstream 'capacityThroughput' has no definition and its name suggests capacity-based throughput, so they cannot be confirmed as the same concept and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the capacity measured at commissioning, whereas the upstream term refers to a general rated (nominal) capacity.
findings.qaRationale: Both are battery capacity properties of the same kind, but ours is the measured at-commissioning value while upstream is the nominal rated capacity, so they overlap without one strictly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: The upstream property denotes general fuel or battery capacity, while the OUR term specifies initial capacity at commissioning.
findings.qaRationale: schema:fuelCapacity is a vehicle-level property denoting total fuel-tank or battery storage of a car, not a battery's measured capacity at commissioning, so they are not the same concept and not in a genuine subsumption relation.

findings.rationale: Our property denotes a general capacity at commissioning, while the candidate refers specifically to seating capacity for persons.
findings.qaRationale: Battery electrical capacity at commissioning and the number of seats in a vehicle are unrelated concepts despite both being numeric capacities, so no SKOS mapping holds.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the declaration of recycled material content for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are recycledContent properties about declaring recycled material, but the upstream oec term has no definition and ours adds a battery-specific structured range with material-type and pre/post-consumer breakdown, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: Our property declares recycled content with detailed breakdown, while the candidate represents a specific numeric fraction of recycled mass.
findings.qaRationale: Our property carries a structured RecycledContent object with per-material and pre/post-consumer breakdown, but the UNTP candidate is a single recycled mass fraction value, so ours expresses a more specific/richer concept rather than a broader superset.

findings.rationale: Our class describes a specific adverse battery event, whereas the candidate is a general business event.
findings.qaRationale: A physical adverse incident affecting a battery (accident, damage, safety) is a different concept from a business-lifecycle event that fulfils a business's needs or legal obligations, so it is not a narrower kind of it.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies adverse battery events, whereas the candidate is a generic event related to public services.
findings.qaRationale: The SEMICeu m8g:Event is scoped to events related to a Public Service, not a generic event class, so an adverse battery incident is not a narrower kind of it but a different concept entirely.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to any service website, whereas the OUR term specifies a URL listing authorized service centers.
findings.qaRationale: schema:serviceUrl is a ServiceChannel property for the website used to access a service online, whereas our term is a URL pointing to a directory of authorized physical service centers (repair locations) — different concepts, not a genus-species specialization.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to a general maximum power rating, whereas the OUR term specifies power capability at particular state‑of‑charge levels.
findings.qaRationale: Original power capability measured at specific SoC levels (80%/20%) is a distinct battery degradation-reference metric, not a narrower form of a single nameplate maximum-power rating, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The candidate property denotes a general maximum permitted power, whereas our term specifies the original power capability at particular state‑of‑charge levels.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured original-power-capability-at-SoC characteristic (range PowerCapabilityAtSoC) measured at 80%/20% SoC, which is a different concept from the upstream's general maximum-permitted-power limit, not merely a narrower version of it.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to a self‑discharge current, whereas the OUR term specifies a percentage rate per month.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties characterize a battery's self-discharge phenomenon, but ours quantifies it as a percentage rate per month while the upstream term frames it as a current, so they overlap conceptually without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the rate at which a battery loses charge over time, though the candidate term lacks explicit unit or monthly percentage specification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are battery self-discharge properties, but 'evolution' connotes a change-over-time trajectory rather than our single instantaneous percent-per-month rate, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a chemical component, but the candidate is a general active ingredient while our term specifies anode material in battery chemistry.
findings.qaRationale: schema:activeIngredient denotes the pharmacologically/biologically active component of drugs or dietary supplements, an unrelated domain from a battery's anode chemical composition, so the only overlap is the superficial word 'active'.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes overall battery chemistry, while the OUR term specifies only the anode’s active material composition.
findings.qaRationale: Anode active material composition is a partitive component of battery chemistry, not a narrower kind of it, and SKOS narrowMatch encodes hierarchical generalization rather than part-whole, so with the candidate undefined these are distinct concepts.

findings.rationale: Both refer to an identifier for a party/party location, but our term is specifically the economic operator’s identifier whereas GLN is a generic identification key.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a scheme-agnostic economic-operator identifier slot (admitting EOID, GLN, or others), not the GS1 GLN concept itself — GLN is one concrete identification key that also covers physical locations, so neither subsumes the other and they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The OUR property represents any economic operator identifier, whereas the GS1 GLN is a specific type of such identifier.
findings.qaRationale: Our generic economic-operator identifier (any scheme: GLN, EOID, etc.) overlaps with gs1:gln but does not cleanly contain it, since GLN primarily identifies locations/parties rather than economic operators specifically, so the concepts overlap without a strict hierarchy.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named documentUrl and represent a URL pointing to a document, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a URL pointing to a document and share range xsd:anyURI, but the upstream core property has no stated definition and is undomained while ours is scoped to eubat:DismantlingDocument, so identity cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to any internet resource, while the OUR term is specifically a URL pointing to a document.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties holding a URL to a referenced resource, but ours is constrained to a document on a DismantlingDocument while gs1:referencedFileURL covers any internet resource, making ours the narrower term.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a URL, but our property is specific to a dismantling document whereas schema.org’s url is generic for any item.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a URL, but schema.org's url is a generic any-item property while ours is constrained to a dismantling document, making schema.org's term the broader one and ours the narrower — so from our perspective the relation to the generic upstream term is not interchangeable but a closeMatch at best, yet since ours is strictly a specialization, NARROW is the honest grade.

findings.rationale: The candidate property is a generic URL reference, whereas the OUR term specifically denotes a document URL within a dismantling document.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property typed to xsd:anyURI carrying the document's URL, but the upstream definitionless 'url' belongs to dpp-keystone and the absence of any definition plus its generic name makes a same-concept match unverifiable, so I reject rather than assert a graded relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL related to a document, but downloadUrl specifically indicates a binary download link whereas our property is a general reference URL.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a generic reference URL to a document, whereas schema:downloadUrl specifically denotes a binary download link (better matched by schema:url/contentUrl), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the total carbon footprint expressed in kg CO₂e, with identical scope and units.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a total (non-normalized) carbon footprint, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical units/scope, so EXACT cannot be safely asserted.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to the carbon footprint of production activities, which is a subset of the total absolute carbon footprint represented by our term.
findings.qaRationale: Both are carbon-footprint properties, and the candidate's label scopes it to the production stage while our term is the total (absolute, full-lifecycle) footprint, making ours the broader concept—though the candidate's missing definition leaves residual uncertainty.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the total, non‑normalized carbon footprint in kg CO₂e for a product or process.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for an absolute (non-normalized) product carbon footprint and the names align, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical units, scope, or value structure, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to a declaration of total carbon emissions, but the candidate lacks explicit normalization detail and may represent a broader footprint concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific numeric quantity (the total CO2e value) within a declaration, whereas the candidate property denotes the carbon-footprint declaration container itself (our term's own domain class), so they are different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the minimum allowable voltage to avoid deep discharge damage, expressed in volts.
findings.qaRationale: Both are voltage-minimum properties so the concept overlaps, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm it means the same deep-discharge floor rather than a minimum operating/rated voltage, so EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: The schema.org property represents a generic product model identifier, while our term is a battery‑specific identifier required by regulation.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing a product's model identifier; schema:model is the generic product-model property and our term is a battery-specific, regulation-mandated narrowing of it.

findings.rationale: Our property points to a specific measurement certificate document, whereas the candidate refers to the issuing agency’s URL.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the URL of a specific measurement certificate document, whereas the candidate is the URL of the agency that issues a certification standard; these are distinct referents with no genuine subsumption.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property is a general link to certification information, whereas our term specifies a URL for a measurement certificate document.
findings.qaRationale: A measurement certificate documents measured values (a test/measurement record), which is a distinct concept from GS1 certificationInfo's general conformity/accreditation certification link, so it is not a true subtype of it.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property is a generic URL to any internet resource, while our term specifically denotes a URL pointing to a measurement certificate document.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties pointing to a resource URL, and our measurement-certificate URL is a specific specialization of GS1's generic referenced-file URL, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and intended meaning of assessing data quality, indicating they represent the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties sharing the same name and data-quality-assessment concept, but the upstream core term has no definition to confirm interchangeability while ours adds a specific A-E rating constraint, so a weaker overlap grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and intended meaning of indicating a product’s regulatory compliance status.
findings.qaRationale: Our property scopes regulatory compliance status specifically to the battery, making it a battery-specific specialization of the generic core complianceStatus property, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the current status of a regulatory or certification process, but one focuses on compliance while the other refers to certification.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a regulatory compliance-status property on a battery, whereas gs1:certificationStatus reports the active/inactive state of a certification instance; these are distinct concepts despite surface similarity.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the current status of a formal approval or certification, but one focuses on regulatory compliance while the other refers to general certification status.
findings.qaRationale: Certification status denotes the lifecycle state (active/inactive) of a specific certificate, whereas our term denotes whether the battery conforms to regulatory requirements; these are distinct concepts, not merely overlapping.

findings.rationale: Both properties indicate the current legal or regulatory status of a product, though one is specific to battery compliance and the other to drug/supplement legal status.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/legalStatus denotes a drug/supplement's controlled-substance legal classification, not a product's regulatory compliance state, so despite the shared word 'status' the concepts do not align.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the maximum battery power allowed, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern a battery's maximum power, but 'maximum permitted' denotes a regulatory ceiling while 'ratedMaximumPower' denotes the manufacturer's declared rated output, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a power value in watts/kilowatts, but one refers to battery power while the other refers to a vehicle engine.
findings.qaRationale: Schema.org enginePower is the power of a vehicle's combustion/engine output, a model-level vehicle spec, whereas our term is the maximum permitted power of a battery; different concept domains, so reject.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the maximum power allowed for a battery, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both are battery maximum-power properties, but the upstream term has no published definition to confirm identical semantics (declared single value, units, domain), so EXACT cannot be safely asserted and the weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: The upstream property is a generic hazard class, while our term specifies the narrower UN transportation safety classification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are hazard/transport-safety properties, but a UN transport number (UN3481/UN3480) identifies the regulated substance rather than being a strict subtype of an undefined generic hazardClass, so they overlap without a clean narrower-than relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties link to safety information, but the upstream property is more general while ours is specific to end‑of‑life context.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to safety documentation, but ours is a strict specialization—safety instructions scoped to an end-of-life context—so its values are a subset of GS1's general safetyInfo, making ours narrower rather than merely overlapping.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a URL to instructions, but our term is specifically for general safety instructions while the candidate covers a broader range of instruction types.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to an instructions document, and general safety instructions are a specific subtype of the upstream's broad 'instructions' (assembly, usage, etc.), so our term is narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream term refers to general safety measures, while ours specifically points to a URL with safety instructions.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a URL pointer to a safety-instructions document, whereas the upstream undefined 'safetyMeasures' denotes the safety precautions/measures themselves, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both refer to safety information for the product, but our term specifically points to a general instructions document (often end‑of‑life), whereas the candidate covers broader consumer safety content.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product safety-information properties, but ours is a URL to an end-of-life-specific safety instructions document while the GS1 term covers general consumer-use safety information, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the meaning or content of a label, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader.
findings.qaRationale: Both are label-content properties, but the upstream term lacks any definition and 'meaning' (semantic interpretation) is not clearly interchangeable with 'subject matter', so only an approximate overlap can be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both describe a role of an entity, but the candidate is a general organization role while our property specifically refers to economic operators.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties assigning a role to an entity, but ours is restricted to economic-operator roles (a subset of the candidate's general party/organization roles), making our term narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the role of an entity that supplies or operates goods/services, but our term is specific to economic operators while provider is a broader service/goods producer role.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a property whose value is a controlled OperatorRole code (the role itself), whereas schema:provider is an object property pointing to the provider entity (an organization/person), so they model different things despite name overlap.

findings.rationale: Both terms are the same property name and intended to capture the role of an economic operator, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our eubat:operatorRole is a property capturing the economic operator's role and is built directly on oec: types (domain oec:OperatorInformation, range oec:OperatorRole), so it is interchangeable with the oec core operatorRole property it anchors to.

findings.rationale: Both describe an agent’s function, but the candidate is a general role within any participation, whereas our property specifically denotes the economic operator’s role.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting an actor's function; m8g:role is the generic role of any Agent within a Participation while our property is the constrained case of an economic operator's role, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a supplier’s contact point, whereas the upstream term describes any main contact point of a resource; thus our concept is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking an entity to its contact information, but ours is restricted to a supplier organization's contact point, making it a specialization of the upstream generic contactPoint.

findings.rationale: Both properties link an organization to a contact point, but the candidate is a generic contactPoints property whereas our term specifically denotes a supplier’s contact.
findings.qaRationale: Both link an organization to a contact point, but our supplierContact is a role-specific subset of schema.org's generic contactPoints, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: Our property represents any contact point for an organization, while the upstream term specifies a particular after‑hours contact; thus our concept is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a general supplier contact point, whereas the upstream term is specifically an after-hours contact that explicitly directs general contact details to gs1:contactPoint, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the unique identifier of a SOCE measurement, making them interchangeable.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a string identifier for a SOCE measurement, whereas the upstream 'soce' property denotes the SOCE quantity/value itself, so they are different concepts despite the name overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide a unique identifier for a specific entity (measurement vs reservation), sharing the same general concept of an ID.
findings.qaRationale: A battery state-of-certified-energy measurement identifier and a booking/reservation identifier are unrelated domain-specific concepts sharing only the generic notion of an ID, which is insufficient for a SKOS mapping.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the recommended fire extinguishing agent for a battery, making them interchangeable.
findings.qaRationale: Same property name and plausibly the same battery fire-extinguishing-agent concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm full interchangeability, so the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a carbon footprint declaration for a product, making them interchangeable.
findings.qaRationale: Our term restricts the domain to gs1:Product and the range to the battery-specific eubat:CarbonFootprintDeclaration class, making it a battery-scoped specialization of the generic oec: declaration property rather than an interchangeable equivalent.

findings.rationale: Both refer to the battery’s carbon footprint, but one is a declaration while the other denotes an absolute value; they overlap in scope.
findings.qaRationale: Our term names a structured carbon footprint declaration document (range CarbonFootprintDeclaration) for a battery, whereas the upstream property denotes a single absolute carbon footprint value, so ours is the broader container and upstream is one datum within it — but as document-vs-scalar they are not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a carbon‑footprint declaration for a product, but the upstream term lacks a definition and may be broader or narrower.
findings.qaRationale: A carbon footprint declaration is the full disclosure document, whereas a carbon footprint class is a categorical performance grade (e.g. A–G); in the Battery Regulation these are distinct concepts, so name overlap does not make them the same property.

findings.rationale: Both represent free‑text remarks, but the OUR term is a general note about measurements or events whereas Schema.org’s comment is specifically user‑generated.
findings.qaRationale: Our note is a free-text annotation on a measurement/event, which is a narrower use of schema.org's general user comment, but the differing intent (system annotation vs. user comment) makes even this a weak match.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the percentage of renewable content in a battery, making them interchangeable.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties named renewableContent likely denoting renewable-content percentage, but the upstream term lacks any definition or domain, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: Both express a percentage of content, but the battery renewable content is a specific instance of ingredient content percentage.
findings.qaRationale: Renewable content is a sustainability metric for the share of renewable-origin material across the battery, not the percentage of a single named ingredient, so it is a different concept rather than a narrower instance of ingredientContentPercentage.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the organization that performed third‑party verification, making them interchangeable.
findings.qaRationale: Both are 'verificationBody' properties likely denoting the verifying organization, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm interchangeability, so EXACT cannot be safely asserted and the overlapping-but-unconfirmed grade is correct.

findings.rationale: Both refer to an organization that validates something, but Verification Body specifically denotes the verifier in third‑party verification, whereas recognizedBy is a broader concept of any organization that acknowledges validity.
findings.qaRationale: recognizedBy denotes an organization that endorses or accredits a credential's validity, not the verifier that actually performed a third-party verification act, so they are different concepts despite both involving an organization.

findings.rationale: The upstream property represents recycled content in general, while the OUR term specifies a particular share of post‑consumer recycled lithium.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures the post-consumer recycled share of a single material (lithium), a specialized subset of the upstream general recycled-content property, so ours is narrower, not broader.

findings.rationale: Both represent a fraction of recycled material, but our property is specific to lithium post‑consumer content whereas the upstream term is a generic recycled mass fraction.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a substance-specific (lithium) post-consumer recycled share scoped to battery RecycledContent, a distinct narrower measurement than UNTP's generic material recycledMassFraction, so it is not the same concept and should be anchored via skos:broadMatch on the upstream rather than graded as a SKOS equivalence.

findings.rationale: The upstream property represents only a single lower temperature limit, whereas the OUR term covers an entire storage temperature range (both limits).
findings.qaRationale: The upstream GS1 property concerns the optimum drinking temperature of consumable beverages, an entirely different concept from a battery's allowable storage temperature range, sharing only the word 'temperature'.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a textual description of the hazard impact for a hazardous substance, sharing identical domain and range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are textual hazard-impact properties with matching names, but the upstream core term lacks a definition and ours is domain-scoped to eubat:HazardousSubstance, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is safer.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote a textual description of the impact caused by hazardous substances, implying identical scope and usage.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties describing the impact of hazardous substance(s), but the upstream term lacks any definition and uses plural scoping, so identical interchangeable semantics cannot be confirmed and a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the CLP Regulation hazard classification of a hazardous substance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are hazardClass properties in the same project stack and clearly overlap, but the upstream oec: term has no definition and is likely a generic cross-cutting hazard class while ours is explicitly CLP-scoped with a constrained domain/range, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the SOCE ratio of remaining to certified usable energy.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream label 'soce' matches the well-known battery acronym for State of Certified Energy, but with no upstream definition to confirm identical semantics, units, or value model, interchangeability cannot be asserted, so the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the due‑diligence information required for a product’s supply chain.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about supply-chain due diligence, but ours points to structured Art. 39 battery due-diligence information while the upstream term denotes a due-diligence report artifact with no definition given, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the battery’s state of charge, but our term specifies a numeric level while the candidate lacks explicit definition.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a measurement-condition parameter (the SoC point at which power capability is tested), not the battery's actual state-of-charge reading the undefined candidate denotes, so they are different concepts despite name overlap.

findings.rationale: eubat:expectedLifetime covers both months and years, whereas dppk:expectedLifetimeYears is limited to years only.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for manufacturer-declared expected service life; ours accepts a QuantitativeValue in months or years and thus subsumes the year-only upstream property, making ours broader.

findings.rationale: The OUR property specifies the percentage of recycled cobalt, which is a more specific aspect of the generic recycled content concept represented by the candidate.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties; ours measures recycled fraction for a single material (cobalt), a material-specific specialization of the generic recycledContent property, so OUR term is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the composition of materials used in a battery, with identical scope and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Both are material-composition properties so they clearly overlap, but the upstream term has no definition and ours is battery-specialized (range eubat:BatteryMaterial, CRM status), so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the composition of materials in a product, and the candidate is intended to capture the same concept as the OUR term.
findings.qaRationale: Our battery-specific property (range eubat:BatteryMaterial, carrying CRM status and percentages) is a regulation-specific specialization of the generic oec:materialComposition, so from our perspective it is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both describe material composition with percentages, but ours is specific to battery construction while the candidate applies generally to textiles.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:textileMaterialContent is domain-bound to textile fiber content while eubat:materialComposition describes battery construction materials with CRM status and locations; they are sibling specializations of a generic 'material composition' notion, not a broader/narrower pair, and neither is usable in the other's domain.

findings.rationale: Both denote a unique identifier for a report, but ours is specific to test reports while schema.org’s property applies to any type of report.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern a report's identifier, but schema:reportNumber is an intrinsic property of a Report whereas ours attaches to a Product and is scoped to test reports, so they overlap without a clean genus-species containment.

findings.rationale: The test report number is a specific identifier for a test report, whereas the regulatory verification number applies to any record or document in a regulatory system.
findings.qaRationale: A test report number identifies a specific test report, whereas gs1:regulatoryVerificationNumber is an access/verification credential used alongside a reference number to retrieve documents from a regulatory registry, so they are different concepts rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: The test report number is a specific identifier for a test report, whereas the regulatory reference number applies to any record or document in a regulatory system.
findings.qaRationale: A test report number identifies a testing document, whereas a regulatory reference number identifies a record within a regulatory information system/registry; neither subsumes the other and they are distinct concepts.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the amount of usable capacity remaining, but our property is specific to power capability at different state‑of‑charge levels whereas the candidate term is a more generic remaining capacity.
findings.qaRationale: Power capability at various SoC levels is a distinct battery state-of-health metric from remaining (energy/charge) capacity, which the EU Battery Regulation lists as separate parameters, so despite the similar name they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the amount of usable energy/power remaining, though our term specifies measurements at various state‑of‑charge levels.
findings.qaRationale: Power capability (the rate, in watts, the battery can deliver/accept at given SoC levels) is a physically distinct quantity from remaining usable energy (the watt-hours left), so despite both being 'remaining' battery metrics they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the amount of power that remains available, but our property explicitly ties it to various state‑of‑charge levels whereas the candidate term is a more generic remaining power concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both describe remaining/current power capability of a battery, but ours is a structured measurement set across multiple SoC levels while the upstream term is an unspecified generic remaining-power value, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Schema.org’s endTime is a general property for any event, while our term specifies the end time of temperature exposure.
findings.qaRationale: Both are dateTime end-time properties and ours is a domain-specific specialization (end of a temperature-exposure interval) of schema.org's generic endTime, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property endDate is a general end date/time for any item, whereas our term specifically denotes the end time of temperature exposure.
findings.qaRationale: Both are dateTime end-point properties and ours is a domain-specific specialization of schema:endDate (end of a temperature-exposure interval vs. the generic end of any item), making ours strictly narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes a general event location, while our term is restricted to NegativeEvent instances and specifically points to a gs1:Place.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing location; ours is a domain-restricted specialization (NegativeEvent → gs1:Place) of schema.org's general-purpose location property, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a place associated with an event, though the upstream property lacks explicit domain/range constraints.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property (event→place relation), whereas locn:Location is a class denoting a location entity, so they are different kinds of construct and cannot match.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named safetyMeasures and refer to the same type of end‑of‑life handling guidance for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Same property name and plausibly the same battery end-of-life safety concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm interchangeable scope, so EXACT is not justified and the weaker grade applies.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general safety information links, whereas our term specifically denotes end‑of‑life handling guidance for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are string/link properties about product safety, but ours is restricted to end-of-life handling guidance whereas gs1:safetyInfo covers safety information generally, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named OperatorInformation and describe the same non‑identifier metadata of the economic operator responsible for a product.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI oec:OperatorInformation is the range class that our property eubat:operatorInformation points to, not a comparable property, so a property-to-its-own-range-class match is a name-collision artifact, not a SKOS concept match.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide non‑identifier metadata about the economic operator responsible for a product, matching in domain and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the battery-regulation-scoped (Art. 38) specialization of the generic cross-cutting core property it points to via its range, so ours is the narrower of the two and the empty upstream definition precludes asserting full interchangeability.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general dismantling information, whereas our term specifies an estimated time value for safe battery dismantling.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific estimated-time quantity, which is one narrower facet of the upstream's undefined but apparently general 'dismantling information', so ours is subsumed by it.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a duration needed to carry out an activity, but our term is specific to safe battery dismantling while schema.org’s totalTime covers any instruction set.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific kind of activity duration (safe battery dismantling time) and schema.org's totalTime is a generic duration for performing any instruction set, so ours is the narrower concept rather than an overlapping near-equal.

findings.rationale: Our property covers the total time needed for safe battery dismantling, whereas prepTime refers only to the preparation phase of an instruction set.
findings.qaRationale: schema:prepTime is the prep phase of a recipe/instruction set, an unrelated concept to estimated battery dismantling time, so this is name/range coincidence not a concept match.

findings.rationale: Our property is a specific textual statement about critical raw materials, whereas materialComposition refers to the overall composition of a product’s materials.
findings.qaRationale: A langString regulatory disclosure statement about critical-raw-material content is a different kind of property than a structured material-composition breakdown, so it is not a narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named concentration and likely refer to the same concept of substance concentration, but the upstream term lacks a definition so exact scope cannot be confirmed.
findings.qaRationale: OUR term is restricted to the percentage concentration of a hazardous substance, while the undefined upstream oec:concentration carries no stated meaning, domain, or unit, so a same-concept match cannot be responsibly asserted.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to the initial internal resistance of a battery, which is a specific case of the general internal resistance described by our term.
findings.qaRationale: Our general internal-resistance property subsumes the candidate's initial-state (beginning-of-life) internal resistance, so ours is broader.

findings.rationale: Both terms share the same name and refer to the remaining usable energy capacity of a battery.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties named remainingUsableEnergy in a battery DPP context and the name is specific enough to denote the same battery remaining-usable-energy capacity, though the upstream term lacks a definition to fully confirm.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the current remaining usable energy or capacity of a battery, matching in scope and intent.
findings.qaRationale: Ours denotes remaining usable energy (Wh) while the undefined upstream 'remainingCapacity' generically denotes remaining capacity (often charge capacity in Ah/SoC), so the concepts overlap but are not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to instructions for dismantling a battery, but the candidate lacks the explicit safety‑specific focus and URL requirement.
findings.qaRationale: Safety-specific dismantling instructions are a proper subset of general dismantling instructions, so our term is narrower than the upstream property.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general dismantling information, whereas our term specifies safety instructions for battery dismantling.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about battery/product dismantling, but ours is restricted to safety-specific instructions whereas the upstream candidate covers dismantling information generally, making ours narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property denotes general safety measures, while our term specifically addresses safety instructions for battery dismantling.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a dismantling-specific safety-instruction URL that plausibly falls under the upstream's general safetyMeasures, but the upstream lacks any definition so the subsumption is uncertain rather than firm.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property provides a general safety information link, while the OUR term specifies dismantling‑specific safety instructions.
findings.qaRationale: Both are link-valued properties for safety information, but ours is restricted to battery dismantling end-of-life safety instructions, a strict subset of GS1's general safetyInfo, so from our perspective the relation is NARROW (the first pass inverted the direction).

findings.rationale: Our property indicates a boolean flag for each material, whereas the candidate likely refers to the broader list of critical raw materials.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a per-material boolean predicate (range xsd:boolean, domain BatteryMaterial) asserting membership on the EU CRM list, whereas the plural-labeled candidate is structurally a list-valued property enumerating critical raw materials of a product, so they model different concepts rather than a broader/narrower version of the same one.

findings.rationale: Both properties indicate whether a material is listed as a critical raw material, matching in scope and domain.
findings.qaRationale: Same boolean CRM-list concept, but our property is domain-restricted to eubat:BatteryMaterial whereas the oec: core term is the cross-cutting general version, making ours a narrowing of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the same classification of carbon footprint (A‑E) and share the identical range type.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a carbon-footprint class and the names strongly suggest the same concept, but the upstream term has no definition and a different label ('Class' vs 'PerformanceClass'), so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Our class specifies a functional category of battery material, which is a more specific subset than the generic Material concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a classification/category concept for grouping battery materials, whereas untp Material denotes a material entity itself, so they are different concepts (a category of X is not an X).

findings.rationale: Our property refers specifically to raw material extraction, while the candidate covers total carbon footprint.
findings.qaRationale: A single lifecycle-stage footprint (raw material extraction) is a mereological component of the aggregate, not a SKOS specialization of the total, so part-whole confusion makes NARROW wrong and the two properties measure distinct quantities.

findings.rationale: Both terms describe the same concept of power capability degradation expressed as a percentage.
findings.qaRationale: Both are battery power-fade properties so the concept clearly aligns, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm the same percentage formula/unit, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe a degradation of capacity/power expressed as a percentage, but the candidate lacks the specific definition and unit for power fade.
findings.qaRationale: Power fade (degradation of power/rate capability) and capacity fade (degradation of energy/charge-storage capability) are distinct, separately-reported battery aging metrics, so ours is not the same concept as the candidate.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a responsible‑sourcing standard, which is a particular type of certification standard described by the upstream property.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a property naming a responsible-sourcing standard (range eubat:ResponsibleSourcingStandard) while the GS1 property names a generic product certification standard (e.g. egg classification); the domains and intended subjects differ enough that this is not the same concept rather than a clean subproperty.

findings.rationale: Our property identifies a specific EU Declaration of Conformity, whereas the candidate is a general certification instance identifier.
findings.qaRationale: An EU Declaration of Conformity is the manufacturer's own self-declaration, whereas schema.org/certificationIdentification explicitly identifies a third-party certification instance registered with an independent certification body, so the identified artifacts are distinct conformity concepts rather than one being a narrower kind of the other.

findings.rationale: Both properties identify a certificate or declaration, but the GS1 term refers to any certification instance while ours is specifically for an EU Declaration of Conformity.
findings.qaRationale: An EU Declaration of Conformity is a manufacturer self-declaration, conceptually distinct from a third-party certification reference, so the two identifiers denote different document types despite superficial conformity-domain overlap.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the number of full charge‑discharge cycles for a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for a battery's full cycle count and the names align strongly, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm true interchangeability (e.g. identical counting basis/range), so a strict reading downgrades EXACT to CLOSE.

findings.rationale: Both describe the same GLN identifier used to uniquely identify a manufacturer.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a role-scoped manufacturer identifier whose range is any string (GLN only recommended), so it overlaps gs1:gln but is not interchangeable with the generic, GLN-typed location-number property.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the electrochemical system of a battery, and the candidate’s label matches our definition.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares our exact name and plausibly denotes the same battery-chemistry concept, but with no upstream definition I cannot confirm interchangeable scope, so EXACT is unwarranted and CLOSE is the strongest defensible grade.

findings.rationale: Battery chemistry is a specific type of chemical composition, so it is narrower than the general chemicalComposition property.
findings.qaRationale: Battery chemistry denotes the electrochemical cell system (cathode/anode/electrolyte type, e.g. LiFePO4) rather than the identity and relative ratio of constituent elements that schema.org's chemicalComposition describes, so the two overlap conceptually but ours is not a clean subset of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and refer to cumulative capacity throughput of a product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are battery 'capacityThroughput' properties denoting cumulative capacity throughput, but the upstream term carries no definition or unit, so identity cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to cumulative energy or capacity throughput, but the upstream term lacks a definition and may be broader.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures cumulative charge capacity throughput in ampere-hours (a battery aging metric), whereas energy throughput is measured in watt-hours; these are distinct physical quantities, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The property refers to cumulative capacity throughput of a battery, which is a more specific concept than the general fuelCapacity that covers any storage type.
findings.qaRationale: Cumulative capacity throughput is a lifetime usage/degradation metric (total charge ever passed through the battery), whereas schema:fuelCapacity is a static storage capacity (maximum amount the tank/battery can hold); despite sharing ampere-hour units these are distinct concepts, not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same label and refer to the expected calendar lifetime of a product in years.
findings.qaRationale: The identical name and fragment strongly suggest the same expected-lifetime-in-years concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm interchangeability, so a weaker grade than EXACT is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the organization that performed a supply‑chain audit or verification.
findings.qaRationale: Both name an assessing organization but an audit body conducting supply-chain due-diligence is a distinct role from a conformity verificationBody, and the undefined upstream term cannot support an interchangeable EXACT claim.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the maximum voltage a battery can reach when fully charged.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties naming a battery maximum voltage, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it means 'fully-charged allowable voltage' rather than absolute/rated max, so identity cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: The functional unit is a specific measurement unit for carbon‑footprint calculations, whereas the candidate represents any generic measurement unit.
findings.qaRationale: An LCA functional unit is a methodological reference basis (free-text quantified reference flow) for PCF calculation, not a coded UN/ECE measurement unit, so it is not a narrower form of gs1:unitCode but a distinct concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named remainingCapacity and represent the current capacity of a product, implying identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties plausibly denoting a product's current remaining capacity, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm the AMP_HR/battery semantics, so identity cannot be asserted and the weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both describe a storage capacity, but our property refers to the current remaining capacity of a product (e.g., battery), whereas fuelCapacity denotes the total storage capacity.
findings.qaRationale: schema:fuelCapacity is a vehicle's total fuel/battery storage capacity, whereas eubat:remainingCapacity is a battery's current degraded maximum (state-of-health) capacity — different concepts, not a genuine broader/narrower hierarchy.

findings.rationale: Battery Status is a specific enumeration of battery lifecycle states, whereas StatusEnumeration is a generic class for any status types.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes and a battery lifecycle status is plausibly a specialization of schema.org's generic status-enumeration parent, so ours is narrower, though the overlap is thin enough to be borderline.

findings.rationale: The property specifies a particular recycled content fraction (nickel pre‑consumer share), which is a more specific instance of the generic recycledContent property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are decimal recycled-content fraction properties, but ours is restricted to one material (nickel) and one source category (pre-consumer), making it a strict specialization of the generic recycledContent property.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a fractional value of recycled material, but our term specifically refers to the nickel pre‑consumer share within RecycledContent, whereas the candidate is a generic recycled mass fraction.
findings.qaRationale: Both are decimal fractional properties expressing recycled material mass share, but ours is a strict specialization restricted to nickel and the pre-consumer sub-stream, whereas the upstream is the generic recycled mass fraction.

findings.rationale: The property specifies the initial self‑discharge rate per month, whereas the upstream term refers to the overall evolution of self‑discharge over time.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a single scalar (initial self-discharge rate %/month) while the upstream term denotes the evolution/curve of self-discharge over time; these are different concepts (a point value vs. a temporal series), not a broader/narrower nesting of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the initial self‑discharge rate of a battery, expressed as a percentage per month.
findings.qaRationale: Both clearly denote a battery's initial self-discharge property, but the upstream term lacks any definition or unit, so its exact semantics (e.g. percentage-per-month) cannot be confirmed as interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The property specifies the initial self‑discharge rate per month, whereas the upstream term refers to the current self‑discharge rate at an unspecified time.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures the initial self-discharge rate as percent-per-month while the upstream 'selfDischargeCurrent' denotes a self-discharge current (amperes/leakage current), a different physical quantity, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: The upstream term refers to any supplies or accessories that can be purchased, while the OUR term specifically concerns sources for spare parts of a battery.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties concern obtaining additional items for a product from external sources, but spare parts (replacement components) are a distinct category from purchasable supplies or accessories, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other and NARROW overstates the fit.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name, domain, and range, indicating they represent the identical concept of a MIME type.
findings.qaRationale: Both are string-valued properties expressing the identical MIME-type concept; the only difference is eubat's domain restriction, which does not change the property's meaning.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the media type (MIME format) of a file, matching in definition and range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting a file's media type, but schema:fileFormat is broader (accepts MIME strings or URLs for niche formats and applies to CreativeWork/MediaObject generally) while ours is strictly a MIME-type string on a dismantling document, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the proportion of material that comes from renewable sources, but the upstream term lacks a definition and may be broader.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about the renewable-sourced proportion of a material, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it is a decimal share rather than a broader descriptor, so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our term covers all renewable material content, while the upstream property refers only to organic materials, making it a narrower special case.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's organicPercentClaim concerns certified organic (agricultural/food) material content by weight, which is a different concept from renewable-source sharing of battery material, so the first-pass BROAD wrongly conflates 'organic certification' with 'renewable.'

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the same concept of a separate collection symbol for a product, with our term specifying it as a URL.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern the separate-collection (crossed-out bin) symbol, but ours is specifically a URL pointer (xsd:anyURI) while the undefined upstream term likely denotes the symbol itself, so they overlap rather than being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL for a specific symbol, whereas the schema.org url is a general item URL.
findings.qaRationale: schema:url denotes the URL of the item itself, whereas our property links to an external regulatory symbol graphic, so a shared anyURI range is name/type similarity rather than the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe a recommended follow‑up action, though the schema.org term is specific to medical procedures while ours applies generally after an incident.
findings.qaRationale: Schema.org followup is scoped to recommended aftercare following a medical/healthcare procedure, not a generic recommended action after an incident, so the underlying concepts do not align.

findings.rationale: Our property denotes a generic maximum temperature within any temperature range, whereas the GS1 term refers specifically to the upper limit of a drinking‑temperature optimum.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the upper bound of a battery operating temperature range, while the GS1 term is the recommended upper drinking temperature for a beverage — unrelated domains, not a broader/narrower version of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Component Location is a specific type of Place, describing a fixed location inside a battery cell or pack.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:Place denotes geographic/physical real-world locations with fixed coordinates, whereas ComponentLocation denotes an internal structural position within a battery's parts where a material is used, which is a different kind of concept despite the shared word 'location'.

findings.rationale: Our class describes a specific location inside a battery cell or pack, which is a more specialized instance of the generic physical place concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our class denotes a functional position inside a battery product (e.g. cathode, cell module) rather than a geographically fixed physical place with extent/address as schema:Place models, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the set of hazardous substances associated with a product, but the upstream definition is unspecified.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing hazardous substances associated with a product, but the upstream term lacks any definition or battery-specific scoping, so identity cannot be confirmed and the weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the hazardous substances present in a product, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the battery-specific specialization of the cross-cutting oec:hazardousSubstances core property (substances in a battery vs. in any product), so ours is narrower rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both terms describe cumulative energy throughput for a product, and the upstream term is intended to represent the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares an identical name and is plausibly the same battery-lifecycle energy-throughput concept, but with no upstream definition to confirm units, accumulation basis, or domain, EXACT interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to an organization that verifies compliance, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader than a notified body.
findings.qaRationale: A notified body is a specific EU-designated subtype of the generic verification-body concept the candidate's name denotes, so our property is narrower than the upstream verifier property.

findings.rationale: The candidate refers to round‑trip efficiency at a fixed number of cycles, whereas our property specifies the efficiency at 50 % of rated cycle life; the scopes differ.
findings.qaRationale: Our property measures round-trip efficiency at 50% of rated cycle life (a regulation-defined fraction of total lifespan), whereas the candidate measures it after a fixed 50 cycles, so the reference points and resulting values are materially different concepts.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to round‑trip efficiency after a portion of the battery’s cycle life, but the candidate lacks the specific 50% life qualifier.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote round-trip efficiency at a degraded life stage, but ours fixes the specific 50%-of-rated-cycle-life point while the undefined 'remaining' candidate is a generic end-of-life efficiency, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the remaining round‑trip energy efficiency of a battery product, expressed as a percentage.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties clearly denote a battery's remaining round-trip efficiency, but the upstream term lacks a definition to confirm identical semantics and units, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is preferred.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the recycled nickel share, which is a more specific measure than the generic ‘recycledContent’ property.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties measuring recycled material content, but ours is restricted to the nickel fraction specifically, making it a narrower specialization of the generic recycledContent property.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a recycled content rate, but the candidate is generic while ours specifies nickel.
findings.qaRationale: Recyclability rate (the share of a product that can be recycled at end-of-life) is a distinct concept from recycled content share (the share of recycled material already in the product), so they are not the same concept even setting aside the nickel-specific scope.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies the electrolyte type within a battery chemistry, which is a more specific aspect than the broader ‘batteryChemistry’ property.
findings.qaRationale: Electrolyte type is a distinct facet/component attribute of a cell, not a narrower kind of battery chemistry (cathode/anode system like NMC/LFP), so their values are non-substitutable rather than in a genuine subsumption relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the standard test used for determining product lifetime, though the candidate lacks a defined range and may be broader in scope.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties pointing to the reference test used for lifetime/cycle-life determination, but the upstream term is specific to battery cycle life while ours generically references any lifetime test, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a URL to a standard test for lifetime determination, which is a specific type of reference file; the candidate is a generic website URL, thus more general.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate is a generic web/file-reference property whose only commonality with ours is being a URL; ours encodes the specific concept of a lifetime-determination test standard, so the match is structural (range) not conceptual.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the same concept of a battery capacity threshold indicating exhaustion.
findings.qaRationale: The names map to the same exhaustion-capacity-threshold concept and both are properties, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it carries the same SOCE-percentage semantics, so a sub-exact grade is safer.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the maximum charging power of a battery, expressed in kW.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a charging-side power limit, whereas 'ratedMaximumPower' (no definition given) conventionally denotes a battery's rated maximum output/discharge power, so they are distinct battery parameters despite both being 'max power' quantities.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a maximum allowed power value, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader or more general.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a maximum permitted charging/power value for a battery and plausibly align, but the candidate has no definition to confirm it specifically means charging power (not discharge or rated output) and lacks a declared range, so EXACT cannot be justified.

findings.rationale: The candidate property lacks a definition and likely refers to a symbol rather than a URL with information about separate collection requirements.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a URL pointing to separate-collection requirement information, whereas the candidate denotes a separate-collection symbol/marking, so they reference distinct concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the energy still available in a product, but the candidate lacks a definition and may be broader or more specific.
findings.qaRationale: Both are product-energy properties about energy remaining in the item, but the candidate has no definition and 'usable energy' may denote currently available charge rather than our 'current maximum energy' (degraded capacity), so they overlap without being provably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the amount of usable energy or capacity left in a product, but the candidate lacks a specific definition and may be broader.

findings.rationale: Both properties use ISO 639‑1 codes to indicate the language of a digital asset.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent an ISO 639‑1 language code for a document.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the C‑rate used in lifecycle testing of batteries.

findings.rationale: End of Life Information is a specific subset of the generic Compulsory Additional Information required for products.

findings.rationale: End of Life Information is a specific aspect of circularity, focusing on disposal and recycling, whereas CircularityPerformance is a broader concept covering overall circular economy metrics.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies power capability at a particular state‑of‑charge, which is a more specific instance of the generic rated capacity concept.
findings.qaRationale: Power capability (kW at a given SoC) is a power metric, whereas ratedCapacity denotes nominal charge/energy capacity (Ah/kWh), so they are physically distinct concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the European Community (EC) number assigned to a hazardous substance.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the ratio of rated power to rated energy (W/Wh).

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the location where a product is manufactured, and the candidate’s label matches our property name.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to a general manufacturing plant location, whereas the OUR term specifies a detailed facility information instance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties asserting the location where a product was manufactured, so they overlap strongly, but they are not cleanly interchangeable since ours points to a structured oec:FacilityInformation card while gs1:manufacturingPlant denotes a generic multi-building plant, and the first-pass BROAD is wrong because a richer range makes ours more specific, not broader.

findings.rationale: The candidate property refers to general location information, whereas the OUR term specifies a manufacturing place with detailed facility data.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:locationInfo means a link to a map/directions/location-related resource, whereas eubat:manufacturingPlace asserts the actual manufacturing facility (a gs1:Place subtype with GLN); these are different concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a manufacturing location with facility details, which is a more specific instance of the generic Location concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property relating a product to its manufacturing facility, whereas locn:Location is a class representing the location concept itself, so they are different kinds of entity and cannot be graded-matched.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the cumulative count of full battery cycles completed.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for the count of completed full battery cycles, but the upstream term lacks a definition so it is unclear whether it means exactly our 'full equivalent cycles' metric, warranting the weaker grade.

findings.rationale: Both properties relate to product warranties, but one indicates availability of an extended warranty while the other denotes a general warranty offer.
